_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams practice 1
questions.prompt: Which stream operation keeps only items that pass a condition?
choices: filter
map
count
toList
questions.explanation: Filter receives a condition and keeps values for which that condition is true. Map changes values instead of deciding whether to keep them.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams practice 2
choices: [Ada, Grace]
[true, true]
questions.explanation: Map replaces each String with the Integer returned by `length()`. Ada has three characters and Grace has five.

questions.title: Trace Lambdas & Streams
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The filter keeps passing scores. The map adds five points to each remaining score. `toList` starts the work and gathers the results. The resulting output is '[77, 95]'.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Lambdas & Streams?
choices: Use a lambda for one small action.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use a lambda for one small action. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices:
scores.stream()
    .filter(score -> score >= 60);
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: A stream needs a final operation. Without `toList`, `count`, or another terminal step, the earlier steps describe work but do not run it.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'A loop may be clearer'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Use a regular loop when the work needs several changing variables, early exits, detailed error handling, or many side effects. Streams fit best when data moves through a short set of clear steps.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use a regular loop when the work needs several changing variables, early exits, detailed error handling, or many side effects. Streams fit best when data moves through a short set of clear steps.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Lambdas & Streams?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Read a stream from top to bottom as data steps.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Read a stream from top to bottom as data steps. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Lambdas & Streams?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Use filter to keep and map to change.
questions.explanation: Use filter to keep and map to change. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Lambdas & Streams?
choices: Finish with a terminal operation.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Finish with a terminal operation. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams practice 10
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Lambdas & Streams?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Choose a loop when the stream would hide the real work.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Choose a loop when the stream would hide the real work. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.
